questions.question: List four types of sensors commonly used in precision agriculture and state their purpose.
keyPoints: Soil moisture sensor: Measures water content in the soil to optimize irrigation timing and quantity.
Temperature/humidity sensor (e.g., DHT22): Monitors ambient climate conditions for crop health and frost detection.
pH sensor: Measures soil acidity or alkalinity to guide fertilizer application.
Light intensity sensor (PAR): Measures photosynthetically active radiation for greenhouse control and optimal planting.

questions.question: Explain, with a diagram, the architecture of an IoT-based precision agriculture system for automated irrigation. Discuss the role of each layer.
keyPoints: Perception Layer: Sensors (soil moisture, temperature, weather) in the field collect environmental data and send via LoRaWAN/Zigbee to the gateway.
Network Layer: Gateway (e.g., Raspberry Pi) aggregates data from multiple sensors, performs local pre-processing, and connects to the cloud via WiFi/cellular.
Application Layer: Cloud platform stores historical data, runs analytics (threshold checks, ML models), determines irrigation needs, and sends commands back to actuators (solenoid valves).
A clear block diagram showing: [Sensors] -> [Gateway] -> [Cloud] -> [Actuator/Valve] with bidirectional arrows for commands.
Mention the challenge of powering remote field sensors and solutions like solar panels or long-life batteries.

questions.question: Discuss the benefits and challenges of deploying IoT in agriculture.
keyPoints: Benefits: Water conservation (30-50% reduction), increased crop yield, reduced labor, early pest/disease detection, data-driven decisions.
Challenges: Power supply in remote areas, poor rural connectivity, harsh environmental conditions for sensors, high initial cost, protocol interoperability issues.
Mention edge computing as a solution for connectivity challenges - local decision-making at the gateway level.

questions.question: Describe how edge computing and cloud analytics work together in an IoT precision agriculture system. Explain with a scenario involving automated frost protection.
keyPoints: Edge computing at the gateway: Processes time-critical data locally (e.g., temperature dropping below 2 degrees C triggers immediate heater activation), operates even when cloud connectivity is lost.
Cloud analytics: Performs complex analysis on historical data, runs ML models for long-term weather prediction, generates crop health reports, optimizes seasonal planting schedules.

Frost protection scenario: Temperature sensor detects rapid drop, edge gateway immediately activates greenhouse heaters (within seconds), simultaneously sends alert to cloud, cloud logs event and updates the ML model for better future predictions.
Benefits of hybrid approach: Latency-critical actions handled locally, complex analytics offloaded to cloud, resilient operation during connectivity gaps.
